description_tesim: Dancers studied with Madame Karinova (aka Harriette Corinne Reinert Steinbeck) at the Karinova School of Ballet, Deauville Club at Santa Monica, as noted in the Ogden Standard–Examiner, 19 Sept. 1954: 25.
A notice for a performance by the Karinova Ballet at the Deauville Club appears in the Los Angeles Times, 17 Jun 1952: B4

Karinova Ballet students, wearing leotards, tights and pageant sashes, pose in a line up behind two dancers. One girl kneels with arms outstretched. She wears a sash that reads Sputnik. The other, in relevé position, wears a Universe sash. One boy, in a button down shirt, dress pants and a sash around his waist, stands to the right holding a baton. Karinova School of Ballet, Deauville Beach Club, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Composite of four portraits of Teresa Kirbe, Los Angeles
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Teresa Kirbe, opera, witch etc.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image is a composite of four images of the actress Teresa Kirbe. In the top left image, she is dressed as a Spanish nobleman. In the top right she is dressed as a Spanish woman, wearing a mantilla. In the bottom right, she is dressed in contemporary style with drop earrings. In the bottom left she is dressed in a historical costume, wearing a crown. This bottom left image is perhaps related to the February 25, 1957 articles in the Los Angeles Examiner, the Los Angeles Herald and Express, and the San Diego Tribune, all of which mention her role in the opera "The Rose and the Ring", in which she played the Queen of Paflagonia.

title_tesim: Foothill Boulevard lined with eucalyptus trees, Pomona, 1929
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 197801979 Pomona, 1929. 9, 6&8. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. One of California's most famous eucalypti-lined motor roads, near Pomona, Los Angeles County. The trees here are 75 to 100 feet tall, having been planted 40 years ago. The road runs thru orange groves. These trees are green all year, especially in winter after fall rains. Eucalyptus tree was introduced into U S from Australia.  [Struck out: 236R1694]

title_tesim: Japanese style flower arrangement by Margaret Preininger, Los Angeles, 1935
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Miss Preininger- Flowers- Los Angeles Junior College. 1935, 2595 P 1 to 21
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Flower arrangement in narrow cylindrical bowl with bamboo relief decoration.  Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at  Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

description_tesim: View from behind tall eucalyptus tree towards road under construction in the Huntington Palisades area of Pacific Palisades. The curbs and sidewalks are visible as well as two street lights. Nine road construction workers are spreading asphalt with rakes and shovels and one worker operates an asphalt roller. Two cars are parked on the road and Eucalyptus trees populate the landscape
Huntington Palisades is an area of Pacific Palisades located close to the 'village' proper, south of Sunset Blvd.

description_tesim: Probably photographed at Children’s Home Society, 919 E. 25th Street, Los Angeles, about 1935
Baby in highchair with tray and blanket, holding toy made of large beads, with railing and trees in background
This photograph appears in Mary C. Kennan's article, "We Orphans are in Demand!" Los Angeles Times, 11 Aug. 1935.
The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911.

title_tesim: Street vendor and Jean Myras outside long storefront bar, Tijuana, 1931
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Tia Juana.  Bartlett.  Outside the "longest bar in the world" at Tia Juana, which is thronged with Eastern tenderfeet during the summer overland vacation season, hovers the native vendor of tourist goods, and he speaks good "Engleese;" will if you desire, escort you through various back doors of the little town, especially when he sees two husky Mexican policemen, each wearing a brace of guns, extra ammunition, handcuffs, blackjacks and other implements of war, in their motor truck, which is a travell-jail, leave for manhunt or something in the outskirts of town.  Miss Jean Myras, Hollywood model, trying her best not to look "professional" in picture.  1931

title_tesim: Mary Loos in vegetable market, Hollywood, circa 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2526 Pls ret negs.  Mary Anita Loos, Hollywood. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Miss Mary Anita Loos, niece of Anita Loos, famous author of Gentlemen Prefer Blondes etc., between classes at Los Angeles Junior College where she is in 2nd yr. plays pee-wee golf in her native suburb, Hollywood. See backs of prints. In one view Miss Loos is shown buying vegetables for household of father, Dr. Clifford Loos, prom. Los Angeles physician. She is buying at one of the interesting Japanese-owned marts in center of Hollywood.
Handwritten at edge of negative: 2526

title_tesim: Photograph of newspaper article, Los Angeles Times, December 12, 1930, Eight-Million-Dollar Fund Theft Confessed
caption_tesim: Text reads in part: Gilbert H. Beesemyer, Head of Loan Company and Hollywood Bank, Tells of Defalcations.  An amazing confession of defalcations amounting to approximately $8,000,000 in funds of the Guaranty Building and Loan Association was made late last night by Gilbert H. Beesemyer, secretary-manager of the association ... He was taken to the County Jail early this morning.  The story of the misappropriation of funds of the association, of which the Bank of Hollywood is an allied institution, came with dramatic suddenness ...

title_tesim: Japanese style flower arrangement by Margaret Preininger, Los Angeles, 1935
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Miss Preininger, Flowers, Los Angeles Junior College, 1935, 2595 P 1 to 21
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Flower arrangement in oval bowl. Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at  Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

title_tesim: Typewritten description of Harve Brillhart's apricot orchard in the San Joaquin Valley, 1927
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: San Joaquin Valley, orchard near Merced, 1927. Apricot orchard. Uncle Harve Brillhart with whom Adelbert lived 1897 to 1906 in Idaho (near Renduck, Idaho).

Text: California, San Joaquin Valley, fruit farm. Typical 3-yr-old bearing orchard, with farm house and windmill, in the Patterson, San Joaquin Valley, region, of central California. This is [where] many of the apricots finally purchased in cans by city people come from. Negative also of "Uncle Harve" Brillhart, the owner of this place, with his pet tomcat, "Puss," who keeps fat on gophers which infest the edge of the near-by barley field.

description_tesim: Boys in uniforms hanging signs. One reads "Coming Golden Rule Sunday."

The location of this photograph is along the  Nahr el–Kalb, a river in Lebanon north of Beirut that flows into the Mediterranean Sea, where armies often carved inscriptions in the wall of the southern cliff to commemorate victories and successfull crossings. There are seventeen ancient inscriptions, and modern ones, including the one visible in this photograph.

title_tesim: Coastal view down slope in the area of the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

View from coastal slope towards the Pacific Coast Highway and beach in the area of the Rancho Malibu la Costa development. There are cars on the highway, which is bordered by a fence and utility pole.

description_tesim: Copy print linked to acetate negative[image ark no. 21198/zz002d45bf]. Text from negative sleeve: 2645 P1 to 3, 10/10/34. Welfare Center sewing room. SM 1934. Marie Finley left. Betty Shoemaker (Right) with blanket. [Negative possibly misfiled]

Copy print photograph of workers in a Santa Monica lumber yard. The men are seen holding hammers and saws. Most wear hats, work shirts and pants; two wear overalls. Four of the men look towards the camera while three continue working. In the yard, wood planks are stacked at foreground while the remaining area is piled with salvaged building materials, including window and door frames (at right). The lumber yard is located near the Santa Monica Ice and Cold Storage Company, recorded as being located on 12th and Colorado, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: The Hotel Playa Ensenada opened on October 31, 1930. Later known as the Hotel Riviera del Pacifico, it was an elegant gambling casino developed by the North American Compañía Mexicana del Rosarito. The architect was Gordon E. Mayer. The hotel was closed and partly demolished in 1964. Partly rebuilt in 1978, the hotel was reopened as the Centro Social, Cívico y Cultural de Ensenada
Artist Alfredo Ramos Martinez, in cardigan with palette and brushes, working on a large painting of a woman with fan and shawl among flowers and vines

From the Alfredo Ramos Martinez Research Project web site: "in 1929, the boxer Jack Dempsey commissioned him to paint murals, create a series of paintings and decorate a chapel at the Hotel-Casino Playa Ensenada (Ensenada, Baja California)."

description_tesim: Cumulus clouds over Fourth Street, Santa Monica, looking northwest from Broadway, with palm trees, parked cars, and businesses.  Signs read: Central Tower Building, Lawn Mower, Locksmith, Laundry, Mitchell's Garage.

title_tesim: Typewritten description of photographs of cumulus clouds, Santa Monica, 1935
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2696 P1 to 2 1/5/35 Santa Monica, Cloud effects, business center

Text reads: Photograph by A.B.  Pls.credit City Publicity Dept.  Exclusive to Outlook.  1935.  Cumulus (Latin for "heap") clouds, gigantic, rounded, billowy masses looking like mountains in the sky, visited Santa Monica on Saturday.  These cloud-banks white against the intense blue of the heavens, as glimpsed when the accomanying photograph was made in Fourth street near the Evening Outlook building, were noted by many students of natural phenomena.  At times the clouds reflecting back the light of the afternoon sun cast a ghostly glow over the city.  The two tall palms at extreme right, incidentally, are among the oldest here.  They stand fully 75 feet high.  Cumulus clouds, say the scientists, are formed by the rising of warm air from the earth, and are usually from half a mile to two miles up.  Other clouds recently noted here are the nimbus, or angry storm cloud; the stratus, lying in layers across the horizon; and the cirrus, thin feathery wisps of clouds, named from the Latin word for a curl of hair.

title_tesim: Sorrento Beach Club seen from Pacific Coast Highway, Santa Monica, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Sorrento Club, Mr. Philbin, 5/29/53, View from sand and highway.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park.

Southern exterior view of the Sorrento Beach Club against the Palisades cliffs, facing northwest from Pacific Coast Highway. A restaurant, other buildings and cars are visible on the highway. The restaurant bares a sign reading "Ne--'s Grill".

title_tesim: Side of house and landscape designed by John Lindsay, Brentwood, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve:"Philbin, John Lindsay, #12624 Sunset Blvd, Brentwood, Photographed 10 AM, May 5, 1953, Adelbert Bartlett, 53515 [?], Santa Monica, Ex 58977".

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photo is a view of the side of a house, possibly designed by the architect John Lindsay. The house is modern in design, with a covered outdoor area to the side. Sections of trees decorate the landscape, possibly used as stepping stones, decorations, or are simply debris. The negative sleeve lists the address as "12624 Sunset Blvd" but the address appears to have changed to "12628 Sunset Blvd".

Building was possibly designed by John Lindsay. John Lindsay was a southern California architect. Lindsay was associated with at least two the firms: Palmer, Krisel & Lindsay, A.I.A. for a brief time around 1952; and John C. Lindsay and Associates.

title_tesim: Painting in an American naive style by Jane Wooster Scott at the Windemere Hotel, Santa Monica, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: John Lindsay...Lady Windemere. 8/5/54.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Hotel Windemere was located at 1431 Ocean Ave. Built in 1909, it was demolished in 1962. John Lindsay, a southern California architect, owned the building from at least 1954 until its demolition. According to newspaper reports from 1954 to 1956, he and Jane Wooster Scott were a couple.

Photograph of a painting in an American naïve style of a city snowscape scene with carriages along a street and blocks of houses behind iron railings. Signed "Wooster," the painting is an early work by Jane Wooster Scott (b. 1929). The painting hangs on a wall covered in striped wall paper at the Windemere Hotel.

title_tesim: Teresa Kirbe, actress, [rephotographed], Los Angeles
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: T. Kirbe, use contrast negative.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image appears to be a rephotograph of a print of Teresa Kirbe. Teresa Kirbe was an actress who played the Queen of Paflagonia, in the opera "The Rose and the Ring", as mentioned by the February 25, 1957 articles in the Los Angeles Examiner, the Los Angeles Herald and Express, and the San Diego Tribune. She wears pearl earrings, a pearl necklace, a scoop neck and a swept up hairdo.

title_tesim: Gordone De Lagniel at the Ebell Club, Los Angeles, 1942

description_tesim: This photograph is probably related to the article "Ebell Juniors Slate Carnival and Dance; Bishop Stevens Will Discuss Shakespeare." 18 Apr. 1942: A6.

Gordone De Lagniel (born Mayes Dickens Donoho and also known as Maggie), was a singer and socialite, active in the 1940s and 1950s. She is mentioned performing singing in various L.A. Times articles during this period. A 1941 city directory for Los Angeles lists her as Gordone DeLagniel, and gives her profession as Musician, and her address as 3100 West Adams Blvd.

Gordone De Lagniel Gordone De Lagniel at the Ebell, dressed to perform Shakespearean songs for the annual luncheon to celebrate the birthday of Shakespeare. She is wearing a costume of a hooded cape and satin gown. She stands on a flight of stairs, leaning against a pillar, one hand on her forehead, wearing a costume of a toga and cape. Behind her is a lit candle. The Ebell is located at 743 S. Lucerne Blvd.

title_tesim: Page of article, “Beirut to Bagdad,” circa 1928
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1928. Beirut to Bagdad. Bartlett story. Touring Topics. Photocopy pages. 5.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of the first page of Adelbert Bartlett’s article, “Beirut to Bagdad: A Southern Californian motors through the land of the Arabian Nights.” The page describes the beginnings of a 650 mile trek through the Arabian Desert that followed an automobile mail stage route. Image at top right is of the Temple of the Sun ruins at Baalbek (Temple of Jupiter), Syria. The bottom left image is of an automobile parked on a street in an unidentified city. The car is packed with people and baggage. Presumably one of the transport men (wearing a fez hat) stands outside of the car. Others in similar garb are gathered with Anglos on the opposite side. One appears to wear a pith helmet. Writing on the car hood list cities along the mail route.

In 1928, Adelbert Bartlett wrote and contributed photographs to an article documenting his trip through Lebanon, Syria and Iraq. The work was published as, “Beirut to Bagdad,” Touring Topics, 1928: 32-35, 60. The magazine, started in 1909 (now called Westways) was a publication for members of the Automobile Club of Southern California.

title_tesim: Palisades Park cliffs and roof of the Sorrento Beach Club, Santa Monica, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Mr. Philbin, Sorrento Beach Club 1952, S.M. Pool.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is an image of the roof of the Sorrento Beach Club as seen from the Palisade Park cliffs above, looking south. Highway 1, houses, another large building, and the beach can also be seen.

P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park.

description_tesim: Photograph of a Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “Lucia di Lammermoor” with Betty Herrick as Lucia  in a bridal gown (L), Giovanni Zavatti as Edgardo (center)and others. The singers wear 17th century period costume, the women in gowns, the men (with the exception of two) in satin breeches and doublet-inspired tunics, ruff collars, and baldricks worn diagonally across their chest (to hold their swords). The man standing closest to Lucia wears a more austere, dark costume with a wide flat collar and cuffs; the singer to his right wears a jerkin, short breeches, cavalier boots and a baldric. The set represents a hall in Lammermoor Castle with walls, stairs and a doorway. Props include a table and chair. Musicians in the orchestra pit are viewed in foreground. Likely performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: Opera singer Betty Herrick performing in “Lucia di Lammermoor.” Herrick as Lucia wears a gown emulating 17th century attire. It is comprised of a full skirt, lace overlay bodice with long sleeves, and a tie around the waist. An organza shawl and bejeweled necklace and earrings complete the ensemble. She stands in front of a background with a painted fountain and exterior architecture representing Lammermoor Castle. The dimly lit stage suggests nighttime. The conductor’s podium, musical scores and a musician can be seen from the orchestra pit at foreground. Likely performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: Catherine Mazet was a Santa Monica Civic Opera singer. Bartlett photographed her in costume as Sandman during a 1957 performance of “Hansel and Gretel.”

Photograph of Catherine (Kay) Mazet and Natalie Garrotto. Mazet wears a tiara, earrings and necklace together with an organza gown embroidered with floral patterns on the bodice. The overskirt has an embroidered floral design with rosettes attached and a sash around the waist. Garrotto’s gown has a satin corset bodice sequined along the front, and satin detached sleeves with gathers along the arm. The tulle skirt is decorated with rosettes. The women are standing backstage between the curtain and a trellis (scenery prop). Likely photographed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: Dean Cornwell was an American painter and illustrator who was prominent in the early 20th century. His works include illustrations for Harper's Bazaar, Cosmopolitan, and Good Housekeeping. He also painted several other murals besides those for the L.A. library, including for the locations: the Warwick Hotel New York, 10 Rockefeller Plaza New York, and the Sevier State Office Building in Tennessee. He is mentioned in several L.A. Times articles, including one dated June 21, 1931, which covers his research process for the murals for the L.A. library.

Photograph of Dean Cornwell seated at a table with a sketch and holding a paint brush and a pallet (or more sketches) in the rotunda room at the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library. Cornwell is seen in the process of planning his mural titled "Four Great Eras of California History" which was to be painted on the walls below the rotunda. Cornwell is looking up at the walls or the mosaic-clad dome. Four women and 1 man stand beside the table look at his sketch(es). On the table sheets of sketch paper, tubes of paint, paint brushes, a ceramic cup holding a paint brush and a piece of fabric draped over the side of the table.
combined_subject_ssim: Los Angeles Public Library. Central Library

description_tesim: Photograph of Paul P. Wilhelm, standing, and Dorie Doney seated between a large wagon wheel and a sombrero on the front porch of the main house, a cabin called the "Vagabond HoOuse," at 1000 Palms Ranch property with a grove of palm trees in the background. The door is covered with a striped Indian blanket and the roof is thatched.

Paul P. Wilhelm owned a property called 1000 Palms Ranch, near Thousand Palms. His address from 1935-1993 was 29200 Thousand Palms Cyn, 1000 Palms, CA. This property is now the Thousand Palms Oasis Preserve in the Coachella Valley Reserve.

title_tesim: Rapp Saloon, Santa Monica, 1950s
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of the Rapp Saloon, a one-story brick structure with Italianate details and a wooden barn house door. Ornate signage marks every function the building has served: “Town Hall,” “Vitagraph Movie Studio,” “Rapp Saloon,” “Gas Light Café,” “Salvation Army”… “Holland Dutch…Repair Shop” and “City Jail.” At foreground, 1950s era cars are viewed parked along the street and in a parking lot next door. In the background, a high-rise under construction towers over the historic structure; to the right stands another Santa Monica landmark – the Georgian Hotel (1415 Ocean Avenue).

The Rapp Saloon, built in 1875, is a designated city landmark. It was the first masonry structure in Santa Monica and remains the oldest surviving brick building in the city. The Victorian Italianate-style building was designed for William Rapp by a contractor known as “Mr. Freeman,” and constructed by Spencer & Pugh bricklayers and plasterers. While currently a venue for spoken word performances, the building once served as Santa Monica’s first City Hall location. It has been a kindergarten, a Salvation Army headquarters, a radiator repair shop, art gallery, and storage facility for Vitagraph Film Co. - one of the first movie studios in the Los Angeles area that was located next door. Original building located at 1436 Second Street.

title_tesim: “Rigoletto” production with Kay Marshall, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 160
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. This is possibly the December 3rd performance with Kay Marshall in the role of Gilda. The role alternated between Natalie Garrotto, Kay Marshall and Diana Guadagno.

Kay Marshall performing as Gilda in a  Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Rigoletto.” Marshall wears a dress with a lace neckline and gold embroidery at the collar, cuffs, and waist. She also wears an embroidered velvet headpiece. She sits on a bench in the middle of a walled garden and mimes knitting with needles and an embroidery hoop. Background flats are painted to mimic stone walls- one with an open door, another with an open archway.  Two performers can be seen from behind the scenery at left.
Image reversed.

description_tesim: Photograph of two cast members with the Santa Monica Civic Opera, in costume as Sparafucile (a hired assassin) and his sister, Maddalena (portrayed by Gladys Andrews). Both wear Romani (Gypsy) costumes. He wears a striped shirt and vest, a scarf covering his head, and an earring in one ear. Scars are drawn on his face and arms. She wears an off-the-shoulder peasant blouse with wide belt, necklaces and bracelets of beads and coins, and a flower behind one ear.

description_tesim: Don Shaw Trio publicity photograph. Don Shaw was a musician who played in different bands in the Los Angeles area, including "The Don Shaw Trio," and "Don Shaw and The Ranch Hands."

title_tesim: “Rigoletto” production with Enrico Porta, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 129
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singer Enrico Porta, in the role of Rigoletto, sings while a crowd of nobles and courtiers look on. The singers wear costumes evoking 16th century attire - the female singers wear long gowns with full skirts and puffed sleeves. The men wear stockings, doublet-inspired tunics, ruff collars, and baldrics (for holding a sword) around the waist. Rigoletto wears an embroidered cape and braided belt with tassels. The stage set represents a room in the Duke’s palace complete with a throne (an ornate wooden chair covered with a canopy, placed on an elevated platform). The walls are built with faux columns and crests mounted on them. Musicians can be seen from the orchestra pit at foreground. Additionally, Natalie Garrotto (second from left) performs as a noblewoman and Jean Kortlander is visible as well (3rd from R).
3726 Additional note: The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. Enrico Porta performed in all four shows.

As a teenager Alicia Mowat won several California beauty pageants (Miss Hollywood, Miss Reseda, Miss Malibu, Miss Huntington Beach, and others). In the 1960's she acted in several Italian films under the name Alicia Brandet (including I ragazzi dell’hully-gully (1964) and Una storia d’amore (1970). She married Giancarl Marcaccini in 1970.

title_tesim: “Rigoletto” production with Natalie Garrotto and Ray Gagan, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 159
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers Ray Gagan (as the Duke of Mantua) and Natalie Garrotto (as Gilda), performing in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Rigoletto.” The Duke (Gagan) wears stockings with loafers, a doublet-inspired satin tunic with paneled sleeves and a small ruff around the neckline. Gilda (Garotto) wears a long, off-the-shoulder gown with a satin trimmed neckline and puff sleeves.  She stands near a bench in the middle of a walled garden. Background flats are painted to mimic stone walls- one with a functional door, another with an open archway.  Shrubbery and vines can be seen on and near outer courtyard walls. One musician is seen in the lower right-hand corner from the orchestra pit.

The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. This is either the November 26th or December 4th performance, with Natalie Garrotto in the role of Gilda. The role alternated between Natalie Garrotto, Kay Marshall and Diana Guadagno.

title_tesim: Political cartoon commenting on oil drilling in the Santa Monica Bay, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Bartlett created a similar image with slight variations (see image ark no.21198.zz002d5qbm).

The caricature, “Juggler Johnny, the Coal Oil Kid,” is possibly a symbolic reference to John Barneson (d. 1862-1941), founder of General Petroleum Corporation. In 1954, a contractual agreement was made between General Petroleum and the Santa Monica City Council in which the oil concern was granted a 10-year lease on Santa Monica tidelands. General Petroleum offered the city $500,000 and 35% royalties from recovered oil. The contract was dependent on a voter referendum that would change pre-existing city ordinances, thus allowing for oil drilling in city-owned tidelands. The amendment proposal lost by a 3:1 margin.

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. Here, gushing oil derricks spill oil into the tidelands and beyond. A small stick figure can be seen at the right of the oil slick, depicted yelling “HELP!” with its arms up. Beyond the oil tides at right are the Palisades Park cliffs and Santa Monica Mountains. At top, a marching band drum labeled “Santa Monica” is played by a disembodied hand. Text reads: “Vote No! Stop ‘Coal Oil Johnny!’” “Beat! Beat Oil. Off Shore Drilling. Ugly Pollution. It Ruins.” Text at bottom: “Stop Oil ‘Bonus’(?)…Vote No… Vote No.” Below these words is an image of a man wearing a top hat, smoking a cigar, and holding bags of money. He is “Juggler ‘Johnny’ The Coal Oil Kid.” More money bags sit beneath a table. Text above that reads: “Oil Agent’s Bonus Table Contracts.”

title_tesim: Political cartoon featuring oil clouds over the Santa Monica Bay, commentary on oil drilling, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: In 1954, a contractual agreement was made between General Petroleum and the Santa Monica City Council in which the oil concern was granted a 10-year lease on Santa Monica tidelands. General Petroleum offered the city $500,000 and 35% royalties from recovered oil. The contract was dependent on a voter referendum that would change pre-existing city ordinances, thus allowing for oil drilling in city-owned tidelands. The amendment proposal lost by a 3:1 margin.

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. Charcoal drawing illustrates a cloud of oil floating above the Santa Monica bay. Text beneath the cloud reads: “Oil Cloud Over Santa Monica Bay.” Farther down the cloud at right is written “Venice” and “Del Rey,” reminding the viewer of other cities that touch the bay. Oil derricks are pictured in the waters at left, while drops of oil descend from text reading, “Offshore Oil.” A large bag imprinted with the word “Bonus?” is tethered with a label that reads: “By Order Moniker d’Oro, The Black Gold Kid.” This is held up by a cigar-smoking man wearing a top hat and suit, standing in a stream of oil. Remaining text reads: “Bondage. Pollution. Ruin. Vote NO.”
For duplicate drawings see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d5q72 and 21198/zz002d5q6j.

Carolyn Bartlett Farnham was the 1964 chairman of Symphonies-by-the-Sea, a free-admission annual series of symphonic and operatic performances. She is noted in the article, "Ballet to Open Bay City Symphony Series," Los Angeles Times, 2 Jul. 1964: WS3.
Two photographs of Carolyn Bartlett, both photographed backstage at Symphonies-by-the-Sea, Memorial Greek Amphitheatre, August 28, 1964.

description_tesim: A female equestrian in the saddle leads a line of riders along the track at the Pam Springs Field Club. She wears a prize ribbon attached to her riding blazer, a bowler hat, blouse, breeches and riding boots. She also wears an armband with a number (3 or 8) printed on it. She is followed by several male riders in less formal attire, and another female equestrian second from last. Judges stand at the track’s center noting additional riders along the opposite side. The fenced rolling hills of Palm Springs can be viewed in background.

title_tesim: “Pagliacci” production with Nelda Scarsella, Harold Reed, Julian Oliver and Sidney Bloom, Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Pace Operas. Cavalleria and Pagliacci. Pace 12/10/51. #15 to 28/29. Nelda.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Production of “Pagliacci,” performed by the Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera. Cast members dressed as Italian villagers greet the comedy troupe who will perform that evening. At center, Beppe (portrayed by Sidney Bloom), holds a flag, while Canio dressed as Pagliacci (Julian Oliver in black and white clown costume) holds a drum. At far right, Nedda (portrayed by Nelda Scarsella) laughs while Tonio (Harold Reed) holds a banner that reads:  “Quest’oggi, Grande Rapresentazione di Pagliacci,” loosely translated to: ” Today, Grand Presentation of Pagliacci.” The setting is a 19th century Calabrian village. Costumes and scenery flats evoke the time and place: women wearing full skirts with peasant blouses; men wearing breaches, short jackets and sashes around their waists. The performers are surrounded by trees, suggesting an outdoor, country landscape. To the right stands a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple with comedy and tragedy masks painted on the pediment. The stage’s interior is obscured by curtains. Beyond the stage, musicians can be viewed from the orchestra pit at foreground.  Opera performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

Leoncavallo’s “Pagliacci” and Mascagni’s “Cavalleria Rusticana” were performed as a double bill at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, December 10, 1951. The opera was produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace. A critique of the production appears in a Los Angeles Times article, “’Cavalleria’ Given at Wilshire Ebell,” 11 Dec. 1951: B9.

The characters are Pagliacci and Columbina (portrayed by Julian Oliver in a black and white clown costume, and Nelda Scarsella wearing a blond wig and satin and tulle dress).  The setting is a 19th century Calabrian village. The performers are surrounded by trees, suggesting an outdoor, country landscape. Pagliacci and Columbina stand on a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple with comedy and tragedy masks painted on the pediment. An adjacent banner reads:  “Quest’oggi, Grande Rapresentazione di Pagliacci,” loosely translated to: ”Today, Grand Presentation of Pagliacci." Beyond the stage, musicians can be viewed from the orchestra pit at foreground.  Opera performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

description_tesim: Production of “Cavalleria Rusticana,” performed by the Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera. Cast members wearing Italian peasant costumes line up across the stage and sing with Turrido (portrayed by Julian Oliver in velvet breeches, short jacket, and sash at the waist) and Lola (Verna Montreys in full skirt, peasant blouse and waist cincher). The characters raise their glasses at a table set outside of the cantina.  The setting is a 19th century Sicilian village. Painted backdrops and sets depict stone and brick architecture surrounding a village square. A church stands opposite the cantina.  Beyond the stage, musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit, while audience members are viewed at foreground.  Opera performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

Leoncavallo’s “Pagliacci” and Mascagni’s “Cavalleria Rusticana” were performed as a double bill at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, December 10, 1951. The opera was produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace. title_tesim: "Ruddigore" production with Mary Jesson, (North Hollywood, probably), 1956
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Oct '56. Mary Jesson, Valley opera, Ruddigore.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 1
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: Formed in 1956, the Valley Savoyards  opera company was devoted to performing works by Gilbert and Sullivan. (Devotees of Gilbert and Sullivan are called "Savoyards" after the Savoy Theatre in London, which was built specifically for the presentation of the pairs' operas). Notices for Valley Savoyards performances appear in the Los Angeles Times for only two years (1959-1960), with performances at locations in North Hollywood (most notably at the Assistance League Playhouse), and one performance at the Encino Community Center.

Mary Jesson, born Ethel Mary Jesson (1917-2006), was a performer with the Valley Savoyards and president of the Gilbert and Sullivan Society (affiliated with the D'Oyly Carte Company and various Gilbert and Sullivan performance groups), as noted in the Pasadena Independent, 19 Dec. 1966:11.

Opera singers performing in a Valley Savoyards production of "Ruddigore." Mary Jessop, performing as Rose Maybud, holds hands with a male performer at center right. Most cast members wear costumes evoking the 19th century, while "Rose Maybud" and two women portraying the "Professional Bridesmaids" wear long, light-colored bridal dresses with veils. Photographed on stage at an unidentified theatre, likely in North Hollywood (Los Angeles) or an adjacent San Fernando Valley locale.

title_tesim: Political cartoon featuring an "off shore oil octopus," commentary on Santa Monica Bay drilling, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The cartoon references “Gen. Pete” and “Coal Oil Pete”– both are shorthand for General Petroleum Corporation. In 1954, a contractual agreement was made between General Petroleum and the Santa Monica City Council in which the oil concern was granted a 10-year lease on Santa Monica tidelands. General Petroleum offered the city $500,000 and 35% royalties from recovered oil. The contract was dependent on a voter referendum that would change pre-existing city ordinances, thus allowing for oil drilling in city-owned tidelands. The amendment proposal lost by a 3:1 margin.

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. The drawing depicts an overgrown octopus emanating from an oil derrick with two of its tentacles draped over City Hall. A sign above the building reads, “Gen. Pete.” The octopus’ other tentacles reach toward an oil barrel seen at foreground. The barrel is labeled, “Exhibit ‘Oil’.”  The building appears to be a photograph with drawings of the octopus and the oil barrel drawn over it; additional blocks of text placed on top. Text reads: “Off Shore Oil Octopus.” “Help Wanted, Rescue! City Hall in Bondage to Coal Oil Pete.” “Get Rich Quick.” “Vote No.” “Vote No Beach Ruin, Downgrade Community, Home Values, Stop City Hall Waste, Vain Promises.”

title_tesim: Clipping regarding dance partners Gower and Marge Champion, 1957

description_tesim: Both Champions were noted dancers, choreographers and actors in their own right. As a team they performed in MGM musicals of the 1940s and 50s. Their television series, “The Marge and Gower Champion Show,” lasted for one summer in 1957.

Clipping with photograph of dance partners, Gower and Marge Champion, performing in costume.  Caption reads: “The Dance- The team of Gower and Marge Champion interpret ‘The Saint Louis Blues’ in a new routine on their show which will be seen on C.B.S.-TV tonight at 7:30 o’clock.” Photograph and caption printed in the New York Times, 26 May 1957.

Photograph of cast members with conductor, Francisco Camacho Vega (far left in coattails), followed by Gene Curtsinger, Nelda Scarsella and Enrico Porta - in costume as Violetta and Giorgio Germont. A portrait with caption, “in memory of…Giuseppe Verdi” hangs behind them. Photographed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.
Related article: Nelda Scarsella writes about her life with multiple sclerosis in "I Live To Sing," The American Weekly (Herald-Advertiser) 15 Feb. 1959.

title_tesim: “O.K. By Me” cast members, directed by Louis Glaum, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum Play Hollywood, 1952. Also Peggy O’ Connell, Otis Garth.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Louise Glaum produced and directed “O.K. By Me,” a comedy written by Sheldon Shedard. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first “vamp” actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.

Cast members from the play “O.K. By Me,” photographed with two unidentified persons. From left: Pearl Jennings, and Peggy O’Connell, unidentified man, Margaret Wells, Otis Garth and an unidentified woman. The room is filled with audience members, presumably, in the background.
This production was referenced in two Los Angeles Times articles: “Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater,” 11 Oct. 1952, and “Beaux Arts Will Reopen,” 19 Sep. 1952.

description_tesim: Seven young ballet students of Julia Stuart photographed in a park. Six girls wearing Grecian-style costumes encircle a young woman who performs a balletic pose. She wears a leotard with sash at waist, tights and ballet flats. The photograph may have been taken at Douglas Park (2439 Wilshire Blvd., Santa Monica).

Julia Stuart is noted as a dance instructor in several Santa Monica directories, dates ranging 1947-1960. Addresses include 2721 Wilshire Blvd. (1954 directory), and 2315 Wilshire Blvd. (1960 directory).
Two additional photographs feature these seven students wearing the same outfits, performing/posing in a dance studio (image ark. nos. 21198/zz002d5wzb, and 21198/zz002d5x5x).

description_tesim: Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 2, Scene 2. Baritone Enrico Porta, portraying Giorgio Germont, sings while surrounded by cast members wearing gowns and coattails. Downstage, an unidentified performer in the role of Baron Douphol (wearing a monocle and sash across his chest) faces away from the crowd. The setting is a room in a Parisian home. The set’s design and props reflect an opulent space, including decorative wall treatments, a curtained window, upholstered chairs and settee, and a chandelier hanging above the center space. View from orchestra pit.

title_tesim: Typescript document describing a photograph of Miss Virginia Tyler, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2813. Miss Virginia Tyler, Santa Monica. Valentine’s Day, 1938. Important, mail return negative to Adelbert Bartlett c/o C.C. Pierce, 1572 West Pico Blvd, Los Angeles.

[Handwritten text:] Pl ret neg promptly to 1572 West Pico Blvd [Typescript:] Photograph by Adelbert Bartlett. Miss Virginia Tyler, popular member of the Santa Monica younger set, insists that some good old-fashioned Valentine sentiment is as popular today as it was with her grandmother. Miss Tyler has picked Valentine cards to please herself, or rather cards of the type she would like to receive, arranged on them on a board, leaving a cut-out of a large heart with her smiling self looking through. [Transcription of handwritten text]: Postal authorities estimate 10,000,000? (and up – you make a guess) Valentine cards are purchased and mailed annually in the U.S. alone not to mention the ones coyly left under Sister Susie’s and brother Bill’s breakfast plate etc.

title_tesim: Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes with unidentified man on a rock outcropping at Ribbonwood resort, Mountain Center, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2803. Ribbonwood Resort. San Jacinto Mts Via Keen Camp Post Office. Wn S. Howell Jr. proprietor. Photos of Ribbonwood Trees, 10/1/37. Dr. St. Louis Estes. Jean Myras.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph of Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes (wearing glasses and a pull-over shirt), with an unidentified man (kneeling, wearing a hat), resting against a boulder surrounded by shrubs and a ribbonwood tree. The Santa Rosa Mountains and valleys can be viewed in the background. Photographed on the property of the Ribbonwood resort.

Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes (1876-1951) was an American doctor and the author of Raw Food and Health. He lived in Van Nuys, California in the late 1920s and early 1930s, and had 3 children with his first wife in the 1910s, 12 children with his second wife in the 1920s and 1930s.

The Ribbonwood resort, located at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains, was owned and operated by W.S. (Wilson Stout) Howell, Jr., d.1888-1967. Howell purchased 2000 acres of land at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains from the Southern Pacific Railroad with the intention of farming, but when the Pines-to-Palms Highway opened in 1932, he decided to open a rest stop to capitalize on the traffic that ran through portions of his property. Soon after, he began to clear paths to trails leading up to the Santa Rosa Mountains and created picnic areas. Rustic-style cabins soon followed. By 1935, Howell’s land was a full-fledged resort. Howell’s intention was to offer a place where those in ill health could rest and recuperate in a natural environment. He believed that the mountain location helped him rebuild his health and that it could do the same for others. Howell sold his land in 1964. It was developed into the town of Spring Crest, which sits within the Santa Rosa – San Jacinto Mountains National Monument.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)

description_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall was located at 4th Street and Oregon Avenue (later changed to Santa Monica Boulevard) from 1903 to 1938

Photograph of Wini (left) and Jeri Knowlton seated on their stallions, Buster and Mack, on the steps of Santa Monica City Hall. Police Chief Charles Dice stands between the two horses at center, while Mayor Edmond Gillette shakes the hand of Jeri Knowles.

The female riders, known professionally as Wini and Jeri Knowlton, participated in trick riding exhibitions with their performing pinto stallions Buster and Mack. The women were Californians who performed throughout the western states - including Nevada, Idaho and Wyoming. They are mentioned in several newspaper articles published between 1936 and 1940.

title_tesim: Mount Olivet Reservoir cave-in, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica Water Works Reservoir Cave in, Olive Hill, 1949. Disaster.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: A similar photograph taken on the same occasion appears with the article, “One Killed, 14 Injured in Cave-in: Laborer Buried Under Cement at Santa Monica,” Los Angeles Times, Apr. 12, 1949:1
Mt. Olivet Reservoir sits below two gated green spaces divided by Franklin Ave. in the Wilmont neighborhood of Santa Monica.

Photograph of a reservoir pit taken after a cement cave-in. At foreground, workers with shovels can be seen digging through mounds of soil amidst reinforced concrete pillars, steel reinforcement rods (rebar), wooden planks and scaffolding. At right, several workers look toward a pair of wheelbarrows being lowered by crane to the pit floor. Directly above stand two men, one of whom appears to be watching the pulley and directing the crane operator’s movement. A police officer, a photographer, and several other spectators are seen standing top-side at right near a two story home; additional neighborhood spectators are viewed at left behind the crane.

description_tesim: A photograph taken on the same occasion appears with the article, “One Killed, 14 Injured in Cave-in: Laborer Buried Under Cement at Santa Monica,” Los Angeles Times, Apr. 12, 1949:1
Mount Olivet Reservoir runs below a gated green space divided by Franklin Ave. in the Wilmont neighborhood of Santa Monica.

Photograph of a reservoir pit taken after a wet cement cave-in. At foreground, workers with shovels and wheelbarrows can be seen digging through mounds of soil amidst reinforced concrete pillars, steel reinforcement rods (rebar), wooden planks and overturned scaffolding. The reservoir’s concrete foundation can be seen in the background, set with rebar pillars – with wooden planks and small mounds of soil stacked near each.  Above, a police officer and additional spectators are viewed atop a mound of soil in the background, buttressed by a reinforced wall.

title_tesim: Beach city exhibit, Santa Monica, 1948
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica, City of. Display of model beach city, 1948. Miss Smith model. USC project.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: City planning exhibit includes an architectural model sitting at ground level; information panels displayed at eye level. Panels describe planning for neighborhoods, shopping centers and recreational facilities. [Header above panels:] Principals of Planning. [Panels 1 and 2:] Neighborhoods: Schools – Maximum walking distance ¼ mile; Maximum enrollment 1000 students; Adequate playgrounds and parks; North lighted classrooms; Outdoor classrooms. Streets – Separation of pedestrian and automotive traffic; Access to schools without crossing service streets; Essential services: Power. Water. Sewers and gas; Off street parking. Housing - Controlled population density; Adequate sunlight; Privacy; Outdoor living; Adequate standards for living. Parks – Open landscaped area; play space for all ages. [Panels 3 and 4] District Shopping Center – Downtown Commercial: Serves two or more neighborhoods; Retails household necessities; Accessible to consumers; Separation of service and shoppers; Adequate parking; Readily expandable. [Panel 4, Downtown Commercial continued:] Centrally located; Adequate parking; Rapid transportation from outlying areas; Pedestrian areas free from automotive traffic; Service streets screened or subterranean; Relationship of functions; Accessability [sic] to freeway.

title_tesim: Crowds walking along Lick Pier, Venice, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2481, 5 negs. Ocean Park Pier. Crowds on pier.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2481 P 3
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Crowds of men, women and children walk along a path lined with businesses at Lick Pier (near Ocean Park Pier). Businesses include Frank’s Place, the Jumbo Parlor, and Lick Pier Bath House. Most of the women wear dresses and cloche hats, some wear overcoats. Most men wear suits, ties and hats or caps. At foreground, a young boy looks toward the camera and tugs on the bathing suit of a man walking ahead of him. The man wears a fedora and carries his shoes in one hand.
Lick Pier was mostly in Venice, Ocean Park Pier in Santa Monica (Cf. http://www.westland.net/venicehistory/articles/pickpier.htm). View is from Venice side.

title_tesim: Beach city exhibit and model, Santa Monica, 1948
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica, City of. Display of model beach city, 1948. Miss Smith model. USC project.

description_tesim: Model identified as “Miss Smith,” wearing a two-piece swim suit, photographed kneeling next to an architectural model of a beach city. The small-scale model maps neighborhoods (schools, housing, parks, streets), shopping districts, civic centers and recreational areas. Panels in the background diagram and textually describe amusement areas, civic and cultural centers, and recreation. Interests identified in the panel on the right are:  Natural site location, Accessibility to transportation from extended areas, Provisions for resort trade and local residents, Segregated facilities for all ages, Open spaces and rest areas in commercial amusement center, Accessibility to beach, Development of beach,and Development of Yacht Harbor.

title_tesim: Correspondence received by Eugene d'Orange regarding possible ancient text, 1931
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2511 P 3
Text from negative sleeve: 2511. Santa Monica. Eugene d'Orange. Eugene Drake, 1931. "Ancient" writing? See story.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Correspondence received by Eugene d’Orange regarding possible Aramaic writings. The letter at the top of the stack was sent to d'Orange by Dr. Sir J. J. (Jivanji Jamshedji) Modi of the Oriental Institute, 172 Hornby Road, Fort, Bombay (India). The letter reads, in part: "I sent your script to the Curator of our Museum here for decipherment and he considers the same akin to the aramaic [sic] characters. I sent it subsequently to a Professor of Hebrew taking the script to be Aramaic and I am sorry my assistant did not..." Additional letters were received from the British College of Psychic Science, LTD, and Columbia University's Department of Indo-Iranian Languages. Of note, a "Report on Enquiry at British Museum Regarding Mr. Drake's Script" (dated November 12th 1930) is viewed at right, third page from top. No other viewable text mentions "Mr. Drake."
May be the same person as Eugene Harry Drake, 1889-1973, resident of Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performers singing carols in a Christmas-themed photomontage, 1955
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1955. Santa Monica Opera Xmas Cards. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photomontage image, possibly intended for use as a Christmas card. The photograph features members of the Santa Monica Civic Opera dressed in Dickens-style costumes, singing from books of Christmas carols. This image has been superimposed onto a photograph of an unidentified, 2-story home with an outdoor Christmas tree. In addition, a brick wall has been added between the carolers and the property, creating a relatively seamless composite. Civic opera singers include: Barbara Ludwig (front row, second from left), June Moss (center), Teresa Kirbe and Enrico Porta (in top hat, holding a small dog). Caption at top right: "To wish you happiness not only at Christmas but always."

The photograph of Santa Monica Civic Opera singers dressed as Dickens carolers was taken at the Bullock's department store in Westwood. The house that was used as a backdrop is unidentified. (See image ark nos. 21198/zz002d6dkb and 21198/zz002d6dnc.)

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production with Natalie Garrotto, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 28
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 2. A male singer in the role of Prince Danilo (Count Danilo) stands at center while cast members watch events unfold. Danilo wears a military dress uniform, while the other men in attendance wear tuxedos or service uniforms. A performer portraying the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari) is seated to the left; Natalie Garrotto in the role of Natalie (Valencienne) stands at right.  They, like the other female on stage, wear the traditional clothing of a fictional Slavic country - including kokoshnik-inspired headdresses, peasant blouses, full skirts with aprons and waist cinchers. The setting is a garden at Hannah’s home. Scenery flats representing trees, a faux brick wall and a lattice-work structure all contribute to the outdoor setting. Additional props include a rattan table and chairs. Beyond the stage, the conductor and musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production with Natalie Garrotto, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 24
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. Natalie Garrotto, portraying Natalie (Valencienne) sings at center stage while fellow cast members (portraying party guests) look on. The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris. The women wear evening gowns; the men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands to the far right. He wears a powdered wig, full sleeve shirt, waistcoat, breaches and stockings. Decorative set pieces with intricate floral designs flank a faux marble staircase. Additional stage props include a table set with candelabra and flowers, chairs and a settee. Beyond the stage, musicians are viewed in silhouette in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Leona Bertrand as a seer, Venice, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2821. Leona Bertrand. 7/1/37. Temple Fires Relighted. Arab costume. Portraits as seeress.
Handwritten on edge of negative: Tree of Heaven des...
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Bertrand was a Cuban born dramatics teacher, residing in 1938 at 115 Wadsworth in Ocean Park. The city directory lists her as a dramatics teacher with Fern Severance (a drama, music and voice teacher). Severance resided (and most likely taught lessons) at 73 Market Street in Venice. Bertrand was probably photographed at this location (see images ark nos. 21198/zz002d6k62 and 21198/zz002d6k83).

Salon portrait of Leona Bertrand dressed as a seer (clairvoyant). Bertrand wears a sheer white dress and veil draped over her head and shoulders, the veil secured by an elaborate band evoking an Egyptian crown. Bertrand kneels on the floor behind a low table covered with fabric. She rests one hand on the table and looks directly into the camera. She is up lit for dramatic effect. An urn sits on carved pedestal at right. Curtains and an Oriental tapestry embroidered with floral designs hang in the background.

The route extends into the distance, ending at a mountain. In the sky above, the sun is viewed amidst a cloudy sky. Illustration signed at bottom left, possibly reads "VP...".

description_tesim: Photograph of beachgoers, wearing skirted bathing suits and swim trunks, walking along the shoreline. Crowds of people dot the ocean tides behind them. title_tesim: “New Moon” production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 12 New Moon
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Negative housed with envelope addressed to Adelbert Bartlett. Handwritten script reads:  “New Moon, operetta, de Parker, 1949.” (See image ark no. 21198/zz002d6p71)

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The New Moon.” A female singer stands under the spotlight at center stage. She sings as a chorus of men dressed as sailors (in stripped shirts, light colored pants, and short jackets) and women in long eyelet sundresses accompany her. The set is sparse, comprised only of a landing with a staircase and balustrade wall. Microphones can be seen at three points along the foot of the stage. Image photographed from the balcony, with a view of the stage and orchestra pit, musicians viewed in silhouette.

title_tesim: "Rigoletto" production dance scene, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 41 Varlova
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: “Rigoletto” was performed twice at John Adams Auditorium during the company’s 8th season:  September 22 and October 27, 1951

Ballet dancers and opera singers on stage in Act 1, Scene 1 of “Rigoletto.” Here, ballet dancers in 16th century dress perform as Santa Monica Civic Opera singers amass in groups and watch. The stage set represents a room in the Duke’s palace complete with a throne (an ornate wooden chair covered with a canopy, placed on an elevated platform). The walls are built with faux columns and crests mounted on them. Additionally, cast members dressed as guards stand holding halberds (battle axes). A chandelier hangs above the stage at center. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.

title_tesim: "Rigoletto" production with Ray Gagan and 2 others, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Text from negative box: Lucia opera
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ray Gagan as the Duke of Mantua with the characters of Sparafucile and his sister in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of "Rigoletto" Gagan wears stockings and a doublet-inspired tunic with a small ruff at the neck and cuffs. Sparafucile and his sister wear Romani (Gypsy) costumes. He wears a white shirt and vest, a scarf covering his head. She wears a peasant blouse, beads, and a scarf around her hips over her long skirt, with a flower in her hair. The stage set is the interior of a dwelling with stucco walls, a curtained window and doors. A cloth-covered table and chairs complete the scenery.

description_tesim: The hotel, designed by Julia Morgan, was built in 1906 - the year of the San Francisco earthquake. The hotel and the James C. Wood Mansion were the only 2 buildings to structurally survive the earthquake. It officially opened in 1907.

Photograph of the Fairmont hotel, located at 950 Mason St. on Nob Hill. The Beaux Arts-style building sits on the corner of California and Mason Streets (950 Mason St.). Numerous 1930s-era automobiles are parked along the streets, including a Yellow Cab with an advertisement on the rear that reads: "Standard Gasoline is unsurpassed."

title_tesim: James B. Lockwood with local children, Santa Monica, 1934
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2635 P1

Text from negative sleeve: From Adelbert Bartlett, 1572 West Pico, Los Angeles. 2 negs. 2635 P1 to 3. James B. Lockwood, Santa Monica, Calif. Served as pitcher and second baseman with orig. Chicago Cubs forty years ago under Capt. Anson. Now a gardener and as enthusiastic about it as he is of baseball. His son will shortly become a professional baseball player with Angels of Pacific Coast League. Lockwood is shown in pictures with a group of his young friends with whom he frequently plays on near-by sand lot when he is not attending to his garden.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002d77mw (document)
description_tesim: Photograph of James B. Lockwood posed with a girl and two boys. Lockwood holds a baseball in one hand, demonstrating a pitching grip. The boy at center poses as if catching a ball with his catcher's mitt. A young girl looks intently at the ball as Lockwood speaks, as does the young boy at left (holding a bat). Photographed in front of Lockwood's garden and home, 1444 Yale St., Santa Monica.
Related typescript document further describes Lockwood and provides photograph date (see "Relation" note).
The name James B. Lockwood does not appear on any roster for the Chicago Cubs (1902-present), Chicago Orphans (1898-1901), Chicago Colts (1894-1897) or Chicago White Stockings (1876-1893).

title_tesim: Sheriff Eugene Biscailuz riding in the California Admission Day Parade, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.
named_subject_tesim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

Photograph of Sheriff Eugene Biscailuz, general chairman of the Los Angeles County Admission Day celebration committee, riding in the California Admission Day parade. Two police officers are viewed in the front seat, with Biscailuz seen waving from the back seat. Their parade car is encircled with garlands, banners and flags – including an American flag with thirteen stars, a stripped banner, a California flag (tail end) and the standard for the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (hanging from the driver-side door). The standard incorporates an American flag and California flag crossed at center. The sign above the standard reads: “Chairman Eugene Biscailuz.” Crowds of spectators are viewed on both sides of 4th St., lining up along the Santa Monica Blvd. intersection. On view at left is the Security First National Bank, formerly located on the corner of 4th St. and Santa Monica Blvd. Businesses along Santa Monica Blvd. include a Southern Pacific ticket office, dentist office, a hardware store, and Irwin’s Drugs.

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.
combined_subject_ssim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West

title_tesim: Santa Monica's host city float in the California Admission Day parade, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

Photograph of Santa Monica’s host city float in the California Admission Day parade. Spectators line a Santa Monica city street in front of businesses and (possibly) residences. Words along the side of the float read: “Santa Monica – California’s Host 1937.” At the front of the float: “Santa Monica Welcomes Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West.” A California flag is mounted below. Flanking the welcome greeting are two statues of Franciscan monks; a model sailing ship can be viewed behind them at center. Perched above these statues is a replica of a Spanish Conquistador bearing a cross.  The date “1542” is viewed beneath it. At the center of the float is a reproduction of the Seal of California, including a live model posed as the Roman goddess Minerva, seated and holding a staff with a shield propped near her feet. The seal’s other symbolic emblems – a California grizzly bear feeding on a cluster of grapes, a sheaf of wheat, a miner with pickax, and ships sailing in the bay – are all represented here. The phrase “Eureka” is printed below thirty-one stars, above the mountains and rising sun. Surrounding the image are the words “The Great Seal of the State of California,” replicating the version of the state seal that was standardized in 1937.

This float appears to have had two reproductions of the California State Seal with two living representations of “Minerva” seated on opposite sides. For alternate views of this float, see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7f52 and 21198/zz002d7fzz.

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The standard incorporates an American flag and California flag crossed at center. The sign above the standard reads: “Grand Presidents: Eldred L. Meyer, Ethel I. Begley.” Crowds of spectators sit and stand in front of the Security First National Bank, formerly located on the corner of 4th St. and Santa Monica Blvd., across from the old City Hall building.
