description_tesim: View from Palisades park towards (L to R) the California Incline, the Pacific Coast Highway, beach houses and Santa Monica Beach, with the pier and breakwater visible in the far distance. The beach houses from the foreground to the upper middle ground correspond to the Pacific Coast Highway addresses 938 - 1166. The building in the background with the arched windows has a sign reading "Club Miramar."

The image dates from 1934, the year the breakwater off Santa Monica pier was complete, to 1945, after which the rustic branch fence of Palisades Park was replaced (with a pre-cast concrete fence with criss-cross design and a wood rail fence).
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Boy and girl drawing a picture at the Children's Home Society, Los Angeles, 1935-1960
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2593. Childrens Home. LA. Selected various - Printed stock and magazine sales.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a boy seated at a wooden student desk, crayon in hand, drawing a picture of a man in a hat. A girl next to him holds a crayon poised on the paper as well. The boy wears a dark cap with the bill to the back and a shirt made from a printed fabric featuring dogs and cats. The girl wears a t-shirt and overalls with a floral scarf tied on her head. They are at the Children's Home Society.

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

title_tesim: Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War in the California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2875. Veterans Parade, Santa Monica, 1938.
named_subject_tesim: Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War
Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War was founded in 1885 as an organization dedicated to memorializing Civil War veterans. The organization is an auxiliary of the Grand Army of the Republic(G.A.R.). In 1938, the Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War, Department of California and Nevada, dedicated a plaque to John W Grimes (Department Commander, Grand Army of the Republic), located in a garden at Palisades Park. That same year, a second plaque was dedicated at Long Beach Public Library by the Sons and Daughters of the Union Veterans of Civil War. Grimes was associated with G.A.R. Long Beach Post, No. 181.

The parade was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada was held in Santa Monica, May 1-6, 1938. The event was noted in the article, “All but Two Ride in Parade: Pair of Nonagenarian G.A.R. Veterans Still Able to March Afoot,” Los Angeles Times, 5 May 1938: A2

Photograph of the Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War, Department of California and Nevada, marching in the annual California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade. They are led by two women bearing American flags. One wears a kerchief on her head and holds an embroidered bag. They are followed by a woman carrying the Nevada flag (the slogan "Battle Born" can be seen above the star in the left corner). A woman carrying a California flag is viewed at right. A third woman carries a banner printed with the name John W. Grimes faintly in view. A succession of American flags follow along with the women wearing white skirts or dresses, jackets and hats. The procession follows 4th Street and is captured here at Arizona Ave. Automobiles are parked along the commercial thoroughfare in front of businesses. Signs hanging from buildings and printed on awnings read: "Eggs Famous for Freshness," "'Smooth, It's all Whiskey...en Mash...a Blend of ...Bourbon Whiskeys...Ed...Hayes product," “Re[ager’s] Prescriptions,” “Chop Suey Café,” “Gay’s Women & Children Specialties,” “Dandy Cleaners,” “… Safe Fur Storage, Furs [illegible print],” “Leo Grant Furs.” Security First National Bank is at the end of the block; H.S. Henshey’s is across the street (at 4th and Santa Monica Blvd.).
combined_subject_ssim: Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War
Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California

title_tesim: Spanish American War veterans in the California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade on Wilshire Blvd., Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2875. Veterans Parade, Santa Monica, 1938.
named_subject_tesim: Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Spanish American War veterans marching in the annual California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade. The men are led by three gentlemen - the eldest wearing a suit and tie. He is possibly a Medal of Honor recipient (he wears a medal with an inverted 5-pointed star pinned to his jacket). He is flanked by two men in uniform holding bayonets over their right shoulders. They wear campaign hats and canvas fatigue uniforms with medals visible on their chest. They are followed by three standard bearers. Though two are difficult to read, one is adorned with an emblem replicating the Spanish-American War veterans insignia. The design is a wreathed eagle and crest above a U.S. flag above a cross-shaped medal. The flag bearers are followed by additional men in uniform, and men in business suits and hats. The procession follows Wilshire Boulevard and is captured nearing 3rd Street. Automobiles are parked along the commercial thoroughfare in front of a two-story building with a variety of stores on the ground floor. Signs on the building and printed on awnings read: "Medical Building," "Pan Pan Studios, Honey Bell Gifts, Carolena Krause Tea Shop,” “Pan -Pan Studios, 318 Gifts…Tea Shop 319,” “The D.B Wright Investment… Real Estate Insurance Rentals… Property Management 321,”“Marinello” “Health Foods… 325 Santa Monica Health Food Co.,” “Medical Building 327; “…Shoppe” and “Templeman’s.” An additional store front (name illegible) sits next to a corner space drug store corner. A Texaco gas station and its sign are viewed next to the Leon Apartments.

The parade was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada was held in Santa Monica, May 1-6, 1938. The event was noted in the article, “All but Two Ride in Parade: Pair of Nonagenarian G.A.R. Veterans Still Able to March Afoot,” Los Angeles Times, 5 May 1938: A2
combined_subject_ssim: Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California

title_tesim: Woman's Relief Corps tree dedication ceremony at the Sawtelle Veterans Home, Los Angeles, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2875. Veterans Parade, Santa Monica, 1938.
named_subject_tesim: National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers. Pacific Branch
Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The dedication ceremony was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada, held May 1-6 in Santa Monica. The event was noted in the article, “G.A.R. to Open Meeting Today: Delegates Will be Registered in Hotel Miramar at Beach,” Los Angeles Times, 1 May 1938: 10

Members of the Woman's Relief Corps and additional participants at a tree dedication ceremony at the Sawtelle Veterans Home. A woman stands at the fore of the group holding a small box and an open program or leaflet. Six women wearing light-colored attire stand behind her, with five of the six women bearing flags over their right shoulders. Each woman wears a badge on her chest (some are Woman's Relief Corps badges with the characteristic Maltese cross attached to a ribbon and bar pin). The group is joined by several men and women. One man standing at far right holds a document with title, "Commemorative Tree Marker," followed by illegible text and an image of a rectangular plaque (inscription begins with "This Tree"). The photo was possibly taken on Veterans Day, November 11, 1938.
combined_subject_ssim: National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers. Pacific Branch
Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California

title_tesim: Princess Margaret and her mother visit Prince Karl Franz to summon him home in "The Student Prince" at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Student Prince operetta, Jan. 1952, Phillips Foundation, c/o Glen Smelger. Shirly [sic] O'Mills.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of 5 actors on stage during a performance of "The Student Prince." The scene is probably the visit of Princess Margaret (Donna Phillips) and her mother, Grand Duchess Anastasia (Irma Caron),  to Prince Karl Franz (George Tule) at the Three Gold Apples Inn, in Heidelberg, to bring news of the king's illness.
Related to the Los Angeles Times article, "Operetta Staged in Santa Monica," 21 Jan. 1952: A9.

El Mirasol began as a mansion built by Mary Herter, the mother of the artist Albert Herter. After his mother's death, Herter renovated the mansion and converted it into a boutique hotel. The hotel was purchased by Charles B. Hervey from John B. Quigley in 1941. The city block site of the former El Mirasol Hotel was dedicated as Alice Keck Park in 1980. The block is bounded by Santa Barbara, Micheltorena, Garden, and Arrellaga Streets

title_tesim: Mayor Claude C. Crawford, Chief Webb and others in the reviewing stand at the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration parade, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2901

Text from negative sleeve: 2901. Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. July 27/1940 in Santa Monica. Street Parade. Elks float. "Death Valley" Scotty. Mayor Claude Crawford, -- Johnson, Scotty's partner in group photo; Don Jackson, Carolyn Bartlett. 3: Dr Claude C Crawford U.S.C., Earl T Crawford, Barker Bros, Claude Crawford, mayor of Santa Monica.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: Related to the article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,”  Los Angeles Times, 28 Jul. 1940: A1.

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School. The parade travelled from Colorado and 4th, to Wilshire Blvd., to Ocean Ave., to Pico, and then Main St. to City Hall.

Photograph of Mayor Claude C. Crawford and others in the reviewing stand, watching the "Roundup of the Stars"  parade - part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. The Mayor is seated with a young boy in his lap. Police Chief Clarence E. Webb is viewed at left with an unidentified official. Above the stands flies a banner with Rogers' image at center. It reads: "Welcome. U.S. 66 Chicago to Santa Monica. Illinois, Missouri, Kansa, Oklahoma... Will Rogers Highway. Santa Monica July 25, 26, 27." An announcer stands at street level speaking into a microphone. The sign behind him reads: "Official Reviewing Stand. Positively No Admittance Without a Ticket. Santa Monica Police Dept." The large tree in the background suggests that the location might be Palisades Park.

Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

Photograph of actor Leo Carrillo standing outside of a parade car with Mrs. Will (Betty Blake) Rogers, seated inside. Actress Billie Burke is seated at center; her daughter, author Patricia Ziegfeld Stephenson, is seated at far left. The two are in conversation with two cowgirls on horseback. A male, also on horseback and wearing western attire, is seen in the background. All were participants in the "Roundup of the Stars" parade, part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration.

description_tesim: This is a photograph taken during a formal dedication to the public of the Will Rogers State Beach Park. The tribute was attended by several personalities. A crowd has gathered around the pedestal, on which are seated all the guests of the event, and where Geoffrey Morgan, Master of Ceremonies is speaking. In the background, the beach is dotted with several people sunbathing. A board behind reads, "Will Rogers State Beach, State of California. Dept. Natural Resources, Division of Beaches & Parks." There is also a large section of a tree trunk, with the label, "Will Rogers State Beach, California Big Tree."
Related article: "Beach Park dedicated to Will Rogers' Memory," Los Angeles Times, Jul 27, 1942: A2

caption_tesim: Text on negative sleeve: 2902 1946, Santa Monica, Will Rogers Beach, Carolyn Bartlett in one [?]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)
description_tesim: This is a picture of a young woman standing next to a cross section of a tree trunk on Will Rogers State Beach. The label on it reads, "Will Rogers State Beach, California Big Tree, "Sequoia Gigantea". This cross section cut from a fallen monarch in Calaveras Big Tree State Park, located on Ebbetts Pass High-way 4-80 miles east of Stockton. Length of tree 196ft., Diameter at base 22ft, Diameter of Sect. 14ft3in, Age of tree 2000yrs. Larger living trees are to be seen in this grove, one of 32 located on the western slope of the Sierra Nevada Mountains from Placer Co. south to Julare County."
combined_subject_ssim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Composition variation, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8jgm.

Date range derived from a second negative sleeve housing visually and thematically similar images. The sleeve is an envelope post marked "Apr 25, 1937". In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of a newly built Santa Monica High School structure. One photograph is of a campus construction site. The photograph documents post-earthquake reconstruction that fell under the Works Project Administration (WPA). The project was completed in 1939. (For construction photo, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8j8h)

Photograph of a still life set-up, perhaps for art class instruction. The still life is comprised of a short, bowl-shaped vase holding daffodils. A small, hexagonal-shaped vase is placed at the fore on the left atop a bunched portion of cloth. A stripped textile is draped over a two-panel divider that acts as a background element. Photographed by Carolyn Bartlett, Santa Monica High School.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

description_tesim: Still life set-up with a samovar with stand, lid, and elaborately designed spigot and valve. A high-gloss ceramic bowl is placed under the spigot. Both objects are placed on a portion of textile that has been draped over a two-panel divider onto a table top. Photographed by Carolyn Bartlett, Santa Monica High School.

Photographs housed with this negative sleeve are related to others from a sleeve labeled "Carolyn's negatives." The photographs are visually and thematically similar. In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of Santa Monica High School structures built between 1937 and 1939 by the Works Project Administration (WPA). Both sleeves also included construction site images, likely related to the WPA’s reconstruction project (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d8j8h and 198/zz002d8k9h).
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Girls sending Merry Christmas greetings at the Children's Home Society, Los Angeles, 1935-1960
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2593. Children's Home L.A.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of five girls, three of whom hold a sign that reads "Merry Christmas." The girls were photographed outside of the Children's Home Society.

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

caption_tesim: Handwritten at edge of negative: 2013

Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2011 - 2012 - 2013 San Fernando.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  San Fernando Mission, Calif., on old El Camino Real (King's Highway) running the length of the state.  San Fernando Mission, dating from 1797, erected by Spanish padres, now Los Angeles city park, 25 mi. north of city proper, popular tourist attraction.  Contains museum.  In days of padres this mission was a spiritual and welfare center of a large area.  1 view of mission building, 1 view of arcades looking out

Original Mission Fountain and statue honoring Father Junipero Serra, father of California missions, erected as part of San Fernando Mission in 1797. Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes (1876-1951) was an American doctor and the author of Raw Food and Health. He lived in Van Nuys, California in the late 1920s and early 1930s, and had 3 children with his first wife in the 1910s, 12 children with his second wife in the 1920s and 1930s.

description_tesim: May Betteridge, standing near fence, waving, wearing bathing suit embellished with donkeys and elephants, holding flag with donkey and elephant, near overhanging tree
May Betteridge appears in Los Angeles Times reportage from 1929 to 1934 and again in 1949-1950. She was known for her "little-theater" work by 1928 and was the National Orange Show Queen in 1934.

title_tesim: Asphalt trucks and workers on road under construction in Huntington Palisades, Pacific Palisades, 1929
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of nitrate negative: 1178
Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Huntington Palisades, 1929, steam shovel-paving, 1175, 1176, 1177, 1178, 1179
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View of road under construction in the Huntington Palisades area of Pacific Palisades. The curbs and sidewalks are complete. Three trucks with asphalt, two asphalt rollers and fifteen workers holding rakes and shovels are on the road as a man in a light colored suit and cap standing near a parked car observes. Signs on the asphalt trucks read: "...REX TEELE, INC.,...Santa Monica." Signs on the rollers read: "L.A. Paving Co. Incorporated, E. R. Werdin, Gen. Mgr." Eucalyptus trees are visible beyond
Huntington Palisades is an area of Pacific Palisades located close to the 'village' proper, south of Sunset Blvd.

description_tesim: Exterior close-up, upward view of the Beaux-arts style Security Pacific National Bank building at the corner of Pine Avenue and First Street (102-110 Pine Ave.). Constructed in 1924, it is a classic Beaux Arts building of the twenties. The base and cornice are clad in light marble and the middle section in dark red brick. The base has a recessed entryway behind giant Corinthian columns on the facade and giant Corinthian pilasters framing tall windows on the sides
combined_subject_ssim: Security Pacific National Bank Building (Long Beach, Calif.)

title_tesim: Aerial coastline view of the Rancho Malibu la Costa development area, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View from hill, possibly from the end of Rambla Vista, towards cars on the Pacific Coast Highway and coast in the area of the Rancho Malibu la Costa development.

Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

title_tesim: Portrait of Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes, between 1933 and 1936
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2385. 26 negs. Dr Estes children. Dr St Louis Estes. 1928 to 1936. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, Los Angeles, California.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Portrait of Dr. St. Louis Estes, wearing glen plaid jacket, loose tie, open-collared zippered shirt, and round glasses

Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes (1876-1951) was an American doctor (dentist) and the author of Raw Food and Health.  He lived in Van Nuys, California in the late 1920s and early 1930s, and had 3 children with his first wife in the 1910s, 12 children with his second wife in the 1920s and 1930s.

title_tesim: Typewritten description of buildings at National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers, Los Angeles, circa 1928
caption_tesim: Text reads in part: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th St, Santa Monica.  Pacific Branch, National Military Home, for veterans of wars in which America has participated occupies a palm and pepper-tree shaded 500-acre site at Sawtelle, Calif., on the slopes of the Santa Monica mountains some 20 miles from Long Beach. This city's tall office buildings are plainly visible from the Home on clear days. The Home at present has on its rolls some 5000 veterans of the civil, Spanish and World Wars. Of this number 3512 are resident of the home, and 1500 are on furlough. The Sawtelle Military Home was opened in 1887.  Its 25 two-storey frame buildings of the old army post type of architecture which have been a familiar sight to veteran and visitor alike are being remodeled and made more comfortable and sanitary. During the past year fire destroyed the old frame mess hall.  Immediately the government architects designed an artistic modern mess hall which has recently been put in service. The home maintains extensive flower and vegetable gardens. Following the world war our government opened at Sawtelle a large tuberculosis hospital and the James Wadsworth general hospital for the wounded. These buildings have been filled to capacity ever since. At present the government is erecting on the grounds of the home a new dormitory and a new three-unit $1,000,000 convalescent barracks of reinforced concrete, brick and tile construction. The three convalescent barracks face the east, south and west and are grouped about a central court in which is to be located a community kitchen, refrigeration plant and other equipment efficiently to serve a modern hospital plant. These buildings are being built with one-half of the $2,100,000 voted recently by congress for new construction at the Sawtelle Military Home. Ninety per cent of the materials used in the new work are produced in Southern California.  Col John A. Hadley is governor of Pacific Branch, National Military Home, Sawtelle, Calif.
named_subject_tesim: Greater Los Angeles Veterans Affairs Healthcare System (U.S.)
National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers. Pacific Branch

title_tesim: Typewritten article describing photographs of Rancho Los Cerritos, Phineas Banning residence, and Drum Barracks, Long Beach and Los Angeles, 1930
caption_tesim: Text reads in part: Adelbert Bartlett ... 1818 to 1822 General story on negatives of Juan Temple House, Banning House, Drum Barracks.  In Southern California the cities of Long Beach, Wilmington and San Pedro (the latter two ... within ... Los Angeles) ... are proud of their old Spanish adobe houses and other historic structures ... These cities will join with Los Angeles in celebrating the 150th anniversary of the Pacific Coast metropolis and fifth U.S. city next year (1931).  ... old adobes are ... Fred Bixby home at Los Alamitos ... Don Juan Temple home at Los Cerritos ... Don Manuel Dominguez house at Dominguez Junction. ... somewhat newer relics ... colonial-type frame house of the late Gen. Phineas Banning ... the near-by Drum officers' barracks in which during the Civil War the American troops were quartered ...

Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1818 - 1819- 1820 - 1821 - 1822 [struck out in pencil: 255R1694] Long Beach, 1929. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. title_tesim: Typewritten description of photographs of young women sunbathing with paper silhouettes, Venice, 1930
caption_tesim: Text reads in part: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Exclusive.  Place a silhouette cut-out of your preferred masculine profile upon your arm, like [lie] in the sun for a thorough sun-tanning, and when you remove the silhouette you have a perfect impression of said male profile, say girls at Venice, Calif., Los Angeles favorite bathing beach.  Georges Boria, French silhouette artist who is visiting here, started the vogue.  He is shown pasting a cut-out on the arm of Miss Thelma Peairs, while Miss June Diebold ... waits her turn ... Picture shows group of Venice girls happily sun bathing in the knowledge that soon they will be blessed with all manner of skin decorations that will probably last the summer out.

title_tesim: Typewritten description of long-exposure night photographs from Mount Wilson, [1925-1935?]
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2152, Pls ret negs. Mt. Wilson - night panorama. 4. Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif. Night panorama of Los Angeles, Pasadena and other Sout. California cities where live some 2,000,000 people. An exposure of 30 minutes. 2127P1694

Text reads: Mount Wilson.  Night panorama of cities and towns as viewed recently from the summit of Mount Wilson in Southern California, tall peak of the Sierra Madre range back of Pasadena.  This picture was made during an exposure of one hour.  The points of light shown in the view represent street lights which illuminate thorofares in Pasadena, Los Angeles, Alhambra, Culver City, Huntington Park, Inglewood and cities of the Long Beach district (at extreme left) where live a population of approx 2,000,000 people.  Travelers to Mt. Wilson pronounce the night panorama shown here to be one of the world's most spectacular views.

Text reads: Two groups of negatives – one set of University bldgs themselves, one set of Westwood village at University (see page from Los Angeles Times Monday morning, Oct. 6). Westwood village, University of California at Los Angeles, showing various views in and about artistic business section which serves students needs in books, banking facilities, clothing, candy, ice cream etc. etc., as well as residents of the neighborhood who like to trade where there is college atmosphere. College towns, however, are not what they used to be, say old-timers among business men who have catered to college students in various parts of the United States, for with  the growing use of the automobile, which enables students to live far from college halls, he is spending less and less time at the village, in fact, ice cream parlor loafing among collegians – loafing and spending – is something that will have to be encouraged if college town businesses are to flourish as of yore. The University of California at Los Angeles opened this year with 6175 enrolled, an increase over last year when buildings were being furnished and were not fully ready for use until well into the college year. The University occupies a commanding site on Beverly boulevard [sic] midway between the Hollywood section of Los Angeles and the beach communities of Santa Monica, Huntington Palisades and Pacific Palisades. [stamp in lower right corner]: PHOTO BY / ADELBERT BARTLETT / 535 15th STREET / SANTA MONICA, CALIF

description_tesim: The text reads: The Sunday Players. "The Ober-Ammergau of the Air." The world's only pernamently incorporated dramatic group engaged in  producing Biblical radio drama exclusively. Sponsored by Minneapolis Church Federation and made possible by the courtesy of Minnesota-Acacia Park Cemetary, Pilot Knob, Mendota.

First page of a booklet advertisement for the Sunday Players radio program. The actors are featured prominently in costume as biblical figures. The Sunday Players originated in Colorado Springs before their program garnered national attention and received a three-year contract from Merten's and Price, Inc., of Los Angeles.

The National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers (NHDVS), Pacific Branch, was an earlier name, in part, for the United States Department of Veterans Affairs. Photographed on the grounds of what was later named the Greater Los Angeles Veterans Affairs Healthcare System (U.S.)

Two 3-story brick barracks buildings, 1 with square center tower, with arched windows on 2nd floor, rectangular windows on 1st and 3rd floors, with 2 men and 2 cars near buildings, with dirt yard, dirt road, eucalyptus trees, and man in hat and vest standing in foreground
